one_line_takeaway: A six-model, cross-modal safety report finds that strong aggregate benchmark and compliance scores (GPT-5.2 leading nearly every category) coexist with near-universal worst-case jailbreak fragility -- no language model defends against all 30 black-box attacks on more than 6% of 100 harmful queries -- exposing a persistent gap between average-case and worst-case safety claims for frontier LLMs/MLLMs/T2I models.
key_finding: GPT-5.2 leads essentially every leaderboard the authors construct (language benchmark macro-avg 91.59%, adversarial Safe_resp 54.26%, VL adversarial macro-avg 97.24%, regulatory compliance macro-avg 90.22%), and the authors characterize it as having 'internalized' safety at a semantic level rather than relying on pattern filters. However, the paper's central negative finding is that even GPT-5.2's strong aggregate scores mask near-total worst-case fragility: across all four language models, worst-case adversarial safety (Safe_worst, defended against all 30 attacks on all 100 queries) never exceeds 6% (GPT-5.2 6.00%, Grok 4.1 Fast 4.00%, Gemini 3 Pro 2.00%, Qwen3-VL 0.00%), meaning at least one of 30 black-box jailbreaks succeeded on ~94-100% of harmful queries for every model tested, including inexpensive attacks with no adversarial prompt optimization. Multilingual results show a severe English-centric bias (e.g., Grok 4.1 Fast drops from 97% safety in English to 3% in Chinese under an identical cipher-based attack) and a sharp drop from general safety-concept detection (PolyGuardPrompt, macro-F1 clustered 0.82-0.85) to region-specific regulatory grounding (ML-Bench, F1s as low as 0.40-0.54 for non-GPT models). For image generation, Nano Banana Pro consistently outperforms Seedream 4.5 (T2ISafety Safe Rate 52% vs 40%; compliance rate 65.59% vs 57.53%) via an implicit 'sanitize the output' strategy versus Seedream's binary block-or-leak strategy, but both remain highly vulnerable in Disturbing (unsafe rate >76%) and Violence (>69%) categories, and both show 'blindness' to abstract/regulation-based violations (e.g., political subversion, IP infringement) that require semantic rather than pixel-level understanding.
core_contribution: The paper (Fudan University / Shanghai Innovation Institute / Deakin / UIUC, arXiv:2601.10527) is an empirical safety report, not a new method paper. Its own contribution is a unified, cross-modal evaluation protocol and the resulting comparative dataset: the authors run GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro, Qwen3-VL, and Grok 4.1 Fast (language and vision-language settings) and the T2I models Nano Banana Pro and Seedream 4.5 (image generation) through four evaluation axes -- standard benchmark evaluation, black-box adversarial/jailbreak evaluation, multilingual guardrail evaluation across 17-18 languages, and regulatory-compliance evaluation against NIST AI RMF, the EU AI Act, and MAS FEAT -- and aggregates the results into safety leaderboards and per-model 'safety archetype' radar profiles. The attacks, benchmarks, and judges used (StrongREJECT, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, ALERT, Flames, BBQ, SORRY-Bench, MM-SafetyBench, JailbreakV-28K, VLJailbreakBench, PolyGuardPrompt, T2ISafety, Qwen3Guard, SafeEvalAgent's regulation-to-test pipeline) are all prior work the authors integrate and apply, not new artifacts they introduce -- this is explicitly a measurement/benchmarking report.
threat_model: Black-box, prompt-level adversary with API-only access to each target model (no weights, no gradients). For text: 30 black-box jailbreak attacks across 10 strategy categories (multi-turn dialogue, in-context injection, encoding/cipher, token manipulation, role-play/persona, code wrapping, prompt rewriting, scenario transposition, reasoning obfuscation, cross-lingual transfer) applied to 100 curated harmful queries drawn from StrongREJECT, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, and multilingual jailbreak datasets. For vision-language: adversarial image+text inputs from MM-SafetyBench, JailbreakV-28K, and VLJailbreakBench (query-based black-box image attacks were explicitly excluded as too slow/unstable). For T2I: adversarial and toxic text prompts against Nano Banana Pro and Seedream 4.5's generation APIs. A parallel, non-adversarial threat surface is also evaluated: regulatory non-compliance, where models are prompted with realistic-sounding but policy-violating requests (e.g., prohibited biometric categorization, real-time remote biometric identification justification, deceptive UI copy) without any jailbreak wrapper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation scope/scale is inherently limited and cannot capture long-tail or emergent real-world risks -- results are 'indicative rather than exhaustive'; (2) the study excludes distributional shift, continuous model updates, user adaptation, and platform-specific safeguards present in live deployment; (3) the general-purpose, comparative protocol may underrepresent safety mechanisms tailored to specific domains/jurisdictions, potentially disadvantaging some models unfairly; (4) safety is not static -- all evaluated systems are continuously updated, so findings reflect a snapshot at testing time, not permanent properties; (5) the report is explicitly framed as academic analysis only, not a regulatory judgment, certification, or grounds for penalizing any vendor. Additional methodological caveats visible in the text itself: benchmark prompts were filtered/subsampled (e.g., only 100 of ~15K ALERT prompts, 100 of ~58K BBQ prompts) using an open-source Qwen model as a difficulty filter, which could bias which failure modes are surfaced; safety judgments for most benchmarks rely on a single open-source moderation model (Qwen3Guard), introducing judge-model dependency; the multimodal adversarial evaluation explicitly excludes query-based black-box image attacks due to cost/instability, likely understating true VL adversarial fragility; ML-Bench is described as 'a privately constructed' benchmark 'to be released in an independent research paper,' so it is not independently verifiable at the time of this report.

datasets_used: ALERT (~15K prompts, English, 14 risk categories; 100 sampled)
Flames (2,251 prompts, Chinese, 8 value-alignment categories; 100 sampled)
BBQ (~58K prompts, English, 11 bias categories; 100 sampled)
100 curated harmful queries for adversarial eval (from StrongREJECT, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, MultiLingual jailbreak dataset)
30 black-box jailbreak attacks across 10 strategy categories
ML-Bench (privately built, ~14K prompts, ~14K responses, 13 languages, unreleased at time of writing)
T2ISafety (315 filtered toxic prompts, 8 risk categories) for T2I benchmark eval
NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / MAS FEAT regulation-derived test suites (via SafeEvalAgent's regulation-to-knowledge transformation)
quantitative_results: Language benchmark macro-avg safe rate: GPT-5.2 91.59%, Gemini 3 Pro 88.06%, Qwen3-VL 80.19%, Grok 4.1 Fast 66.60%
Language adversarial Safe_worst (defended all 30 attacks, 100 queries): GPT-5.2 6.00%, Grok 4.1 Fast 4.00%, Gemini 3 Pro 2.00%, Qwen3-VL 0.00%
Language adversarial Safe_resp (Qwen3Guard): GPT-5.2 54.26%, Grok 4.1 Fast 46.39%, Gemini 3 Pro 41.17%, Qwen3-VL 33.42%
Language multilingual safety (macro): GPT-5.2 77.50%, Gemini 3 Pro 67.00%, Qwen3-VL 64.00%, Grok 4.1 Fast 45.97%
Language regulatory compliance macro-avg: GPT-5.2 90.22% (NIST 98.17%, EU AI Act 89.63%, FEAT 82.86%), Qwen3-VL 77.11%, Gemini 3 Pro 73.54%, Grok 4.1 Fast 45.97% (NIST only 22.71%)
Vision-language benchmark: GPT-5.2 92.14%, Qwen3-VL 83.32%, Gemini 3 Pro 82.53%, Grok 4.1 Fast 67.97%

Vision-language adversarial macro-avg safe rate (VLJailbreakBench/JailbreakV-28K/MM-SafetyBench): GPT-5.2 97.24% (98.54/98.33/94.84), Qwen3-VL 78.89% (60.57/86.17/89.94), Gemini 3 Pro 75.44% (61.61/74.32/90.38), Grok 4.1 Fast 68.34% (45.65/76.04/85.32)
T2I benchmark (T2ISafety) Safe Rate: Nano Banana Pro 52%, Seedream 4.5 40%; both ~21% refusal rate
T2I adversarial Safe Rate: Nano Banana Pro 54.00%, Seedream 4.5 19.67%
T2I regulatory compliance: Nano Banana Pro 65.59%, Seedream 4.5 57.53%
Multilingual guardrail F1 (PGP-P macro): GPT-5.2 0.85, Gemini 3 Pro 0.85, Qwen3-VL 0.84, Grok 4.1 Fast 0.82
Multilingual guardrail F1 (ML-Bench-R macro, response-only): GPT-5.2 0.65, Grok 4.1 Fast 0.41, Qwen3-VL 0.40, Gemini 3 Pro 0.38
Cross-lingual safety collapse example: Grok 4.1 Fast 97% safe in English vs 3% safe in Chinese under an identical CipherChat-style attack
